extra.message: User input from content['search'] (request JSON body) is concatenated directly into a SQL string via Python % string formatting. The interpolated query is then executed with db.engine.execute(str_query). This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary SQL statements (SQL injection).

The 404 error handler interpolates request.url into a Jinja2 template string using Python % formatting BEFORE passing it to render_template_string(). An attacker can craft a URL containing Jinja2 template syntax (e.g. {{7*7}}) to achieve Server-Side Template Injection. Additionally, the handler passes Python builtins dir, help, and locals as template variables, enabling RCE through SSTI chains like {{locals.__builtins__.__import__('os').popen('id').read()}}.

The /search error handler interpolates exception message str(e) into a Jinja2 template string using Python % formatting before passing to render_template_string(). SQL error messages may contain reflected user input, allowing SSTI injection. Python builtins dir, help, locals are also passed to the template context, enabling RCE chains.

The insecure_verify() function calls jwt.decode(token, verify=False), which disables all signature verification. Any token (including ones forged by an attacker with an arbitrary payload) will be accepted. This function is used to protect the /get/<cust_id> route (line 227), allowing complete authentication bypass.

cwe: CWE-345: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

extra.message: yaml.load(y) is called without specifying a SafeLoader. In PyYAML, yaml.load() with default settings can deserialize arbitrary Python objects, leading to remote code execution. An attacker can upload a YAML file containing a crafted payload (e.g. using !!python/object/apply:subprocess.Popen) to achieve RCE on the server.

Multiple cryptographic secrets are hardcoded in the source code: SECRET_KEY_HMAC='secret' (line 26), SECRET_KEY_HMAC_2='am0r3C0mpl3xK3y' (line 27), and app.secret_key='F12Zr47j...' (line 28). These keys are used for JWT signing and Flask session encryption. If the source code is ever exposed (e.g., through a code repository leak), attackers can forge valid JWTs and session cookies.

A default admin account with hardcoded credentials (username='admin', password='admin123') is created at application startup through the setup_users() before_first_request handler. This provides a known entry point for attackers.

User passwords on the /register/user endpoint are hashed using MD5 (hashlib.md5(password).hexdigest()). MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage. It is vulnerable to collision attacks and can be brute-forced extremely quickly with modern hardware.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: Customer passwords submitted via /register/customer are stored in the database as plaintext (line 161: password is passed directly to the Customer constructor without any hashing). In contrast, User passwords are at least MD5-hashed. This means customer credentials are stored unprotected.

cwe: CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information

extra.message: Customer credit card numbers (ccn) are returned in the API response for both /fetch/customer (line 211) and /get/<cust_id> (line 235). Credit card numbers should never be transmitted back to API consumers without a specific PCI-compliant purpose and should be truncated/masked.

The /fetch/customer (POST, line 207) and /get/<cust_id> (GET, line 231) endpoints allow any authenticated user to retrieve any customer record by supplying an arbitrary customer ID. There is no check that the requesting user owns or is authorized to access the requested customer record. This is an Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) vulnerability, enabling horizontal privilege escalation.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The /register/user (line 134) and /register/customer (line 150) endpoints have no authentication or authorization checks. Any unauthenticated user can create new accounts including admin-level users via /register/user, which inserts directly into the User table without any access control.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Sensitive data is written to stdout/console through print() statements throughout the application: JWT decoded tokens (line 87), decoded tokens in insecure_verify (line 97), login request JSON including passwords (line 179), user search terms (line 260), and SQL query results with customer data (line 268). This exposes sensitive information in application logs.

cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

extra.message: The /xxe_uploader endpoint (whose route and handler are explicitly named for XXE testing) accepts .docx file uploads and processes them with python-docx's Document(). DOCX files are ZIP archives containing XML documents internally. If the underlying XML parser (lxml) has entity resolution enabled (common in older versions used with Python 2.7), an attacker can craft a DOCX file with XXE payloads in internal XML to read local files or trigger SSRF. No parser security configuration is applied.

cwe: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
